chunks.text: Hello, everybody. Greg Kokel here at Stand to Reason, and thank you for joining me today. I had a very interesting time, as I usually do on the weekend with a church. Actually, the next two weekends I have clear, which is unusual. I'll be home and I can go to my own church this weekend, but I gave a seminar on Saturday and had a wonderful time with wonderful people. That is characteristic for the great churches and people I get to meet when I'm on the road. I also had a conversation with a young man who was 17 years old, very precocious, and had some questions about a number of things. And over lunch, we talked about them. And he did raise a point with me, somewhat directly, which was fine, that he did not believe in eternal security. Now, apparently, he wanted to knock that idea around a little bit, get my opinion about that, and so we went to a passage, and I want to talk about that passage as we start the show out. And I don't want to talk about it for the sake of making a case for my own view, which is that, simply put, I think that the miracle of regeneration is irreversible. That is, stipulating someone is actually saved, there is no way to undo that. And there's more theology for me that goes behind it, since I believe in sovereign grace, therefore there's going to be security in light of God's choice of us. That's actually not the point here. What I was trying to do is not disabuse this young man of his conviction, and especially, here it is, lunch, I'm giving the seminar, and it puts me in the awkward position of potentially looking like I'm beating up on the teenager and I'm trying to strong-arm him into my point of view, and I did not want to do that at all. But I did want to get him thinking, put a stone in his shoe, so to speak, on a theological issue that I thought, at least at one point, was poorly justified. And that isn't to speak to the whole issue, which is what I told him. I'm not trying to change your mind on this issue, but I think the proof text that you're using is not a good text to make your point. I think you're misunderstanding the point. And so, when I go to this text, and just to bring you up to speed, it's in Hebrews 10:26-27, maybe 28. What I want to do with the text, and what I wanted to do with him, is to help him understand some important principles about how to read Scripture, so as not to make a mistake. And in this case, I think he was misreading the text. So, let me read the text to you, and for some of you, this is a text you're very familiar with if you traffic in this particular idea. And what the text says, for if we go on sinning willfully after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins, but a rather a terrifying expectation of judgment and the fury of a fire which will consume the adversaries. Anyone who has set aside the law of Moses dies without mercy on the testimony of two or three witnesses. How much severer punishment do you think he will deserve who has trampled underfoot the Son of God and has regarded as unclean the blood of the covenant by which he was sanctified and has insulted the spirit of grace? Now, to this young man, that all seemed to fit together. If continual sin disqualifies one from salvation because the sacrifice of Christ will no longer avail for him, and the reason is, is he is, by continuing in sin, he is trampling underfoot the Son of God, he has regarded as unclean the blood of the covenant, and he has insulted the spirit of grace. This is the language of the text. And that all seemed to make sense as a coherent whole to him, which is why he offered the passage to me as evidence that one can lose their salvation.
